topic: A person finds out their biography was written without their knowledge
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elara traced the ink on the brittle parchment, the paper whispering under her touch like dry leaves. The dates, the accomplishments, the even the seemingly intimate details of her life—they weren't hers. A cold, creeping sensation began in her stomach, tightening with every deciphered sentence. She reread the passage describing her childhood, the vivid descriptions of her early struggles, and a tremor ran through her hands, making the fine script jump. Someone had curated this narrative, a complete, polished fiction. When she finally looked up, the quiet study room felt suddenly cavernous, the air too thin to breathe. Her breath hitched, shallow and quick. She stood up too fast, knocking a stack of books to the floor with a muffled thud, and stumbled backward, pressing the cool wood of the bookshelf against her back, wishing desperately for an exit.

[story 2]

Mark stared at the screen, his knuckles white where he gripped the mouse. The document materialized slowly, a cascade of carefully constructed memories detailing his career, his relationships, his very soul. Each paragraph was a meticulously crafted lie, a performance he had never consented to. A sudden, sharp intake of air caught in his chest, stealing his ability to draw a full breath. He squeezed his eyes shut, trying to force the images away, but they clung to his vision—faces he didn't recognize, events that felt deeply personal yet utterly alien. His jaw locked so tight a dull ache bloomed behind his molars. He began to pace the small apartment, his footsteps echoing unnervingly on the hardwood floor, stopping abruptly by the window, peering out into the indifferent city lights as if searching for an escape route that simply didn't exist.

[story 3]

When the solicitor slid the bound volume across the mahogany desk, Liam felt the blood drain from his face. The text spoke of his triumphs, his quiet virtues, the very essence of who he believed himself to be, yet the voice behind the words was a stranger's. He picked up the pen, his fingers slick with sudden perspiration, and tried to scan the lines for any inconsistency, any misplaced detail that might betray the fabrication. A wave of intense nausea washed over him; his vision blurred at the edges. He set the document down with a violent jerk, the sound sharp in the silence of the office. Liam leaned back in his chair, his posture rigid, his gaze fixed on a point just beyond the wall, his entire body vibrating with a profound sense of disorientation and utter vulnerability.
